text: The surgeon removes the right external iliac, internal iliac, and obturator lymph nodes using the DN block technique, demonstrating the systematic approach to pelvic lymphadenectomy.
The surgeon repeats the removal of the left external iliac, internal iliac, and obturator lymph nodes using the DN block technique to complete the pelvic lymphadenectomy.
The surgeon places an optical ten-millimeter trocar in the umbilicus, preparing for laparoscopic access to the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon identifies the caudal limit of the incision at the parietal origin of the round ligament, marking the boundary for the surgical approach.
The surgeon identifies the cephalic limit of the incision at the point where the gonadal vessels cross the external iliac vessels.
The surgeon makes an incision laterally to the parietal part of the broad ligament, positioned above the external iliac vessels.
The surgeon identifies the course of both ureters transperitoneally, ensuring their protection during the subsequent dissection.
The surgeon dissects the psoas muscle, carefully following the epimysium layer of the muscle during the third step of the surgery.
The surgeon identifies the deep circumflex vein as the limit for the caudal dissection, marking the boundary for further surgical exploration.
The surgeon identifies the obturator and internal iliac lymph nodes as prevalent sites for metastasis, halting dissection at the deep circumflex vein.
The surgeon identifies the cephalic limit of the psoas muscle dissection at the bifurcation of the common iliac artery, marking the upper boundary of the dissection area.
The surgeon dissects the lateral pelvic sidewall, progressing towards the puborectalis muscle.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the paravesical fossa and the medial side of the external iliac vessels using the classic laparoscopic approach.
The surgeon identifies and carefully preserves the genitofemoral nerve during the dissection of the lateral pelvic sidewall.
The surgeon identifies the genitofemoral nerve, which is located more medially in this case, during the dissection of the pelvic sidewall.
The surgeon dissects medially from the surface of the muscle to reach the lateral side of the external iliac vessels, carefully navigating the anatomical structures.

The surgeon pulls the block of lymph nodes medially, creating a space between the lateral side of the external iliac vessels and the psoas and obturator muscles, progressing until the obturator nerve is visually identified.
The surgeon performs deep dissection to create a window beneath the external iliac vessels, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon prepares the lateral meridian of the lymph node packet, positioning it for subsequent removal, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon mobilizes the lymph nodes located between the external iliac vessels, preparing them for subsequent removal.

The surgeon pulls the external iliac vessels laterally to facilitate the dissection of the medial meridian of the lymph nodes packet, creating a clear separation between the vessels and the lymphatic tissue.
The surgeon dissects the posterior side of the iliac vessels, creating a window under the iliac vein to access the previously dissected area lateral to the iliac vessels and obturator nerve.
The surgeon carefully dissects the medial side of the external iliac vessels, ensuring to avoid injury to the ureter, which is visible distending onto the posterior leaf of the broad ligament.
The surgeon completes the dissection, fully separating the medial and lateral meridians of the lymph node packet from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon dissects the paravesical fossa, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues as part of the lymph node dissection process.
The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery as a key landmark within the dissected space, providing orientation for further surgical navigation.
The surgeon halts dissection upon reaching the puborectalis muscle at the bottom of the surgical space, marking the endpoint of the current dissection phase.
The surgeon reveals the obturator nerve within the properly developed paravesical space, highlighting its clear visibility against the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues dissection along the posterior aspect of the umbilical artery and internal iliac artery, revealing these structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon develops the operative area in the extraperitoneal space, identifying anatomical landmarks before proceeding with lymph node dissection.
The surgeon laterally mobilizes the obturator, internal, and external iliac lymph nodes by dissecting the medial and lateral margins of the lymph node packet, without removing any lymph nodes.
The surgeon sections the distal caudal margin of the lymph node packet, exposing the pectineal ligament and coronamortis veins in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies and preserves the previously dissected obturator nerve, ensuring it remains intact amidst the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon achieves hemostasis in the operated area by coagulating the lymphatic vessels, ensuring control of bleeding within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs obturator nerve stripping, carefully manipulating the nerve within the surgical field.
The surgeon gently detaches the lymph nodes from the obturator nerve, ensuring careful separation to preserve the nerve's integrity.
The surgeon sections the proximal margin of the lymph node packet, revealing the bifurcation of the common iliac artery.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to dissect tissues and lymph nodes, achieving hemostasis and closure of lymphatic vessels, which may reduce lymphocytic formation.
